title: A Self-Contained Canvas Page
html:
Zero install, one file

The browser is the most widely deployed game runtime on earth. There is no SDK to download, no license, no build step required to get pixels on screen. You write an HTML file, double-click it, and it runs. In this lesson we build the smallest possible foundation: a single page with a <canvas> element and the JavaScript that grabs its 2D drawing context.

A canvas is a rectangular bitmap you draw into with code. It has no shapes, layers, or memory of what you drew — every frame you paint pixels, and they stay until you paint over them. That simplicity is exactly why it is great for learning game dev: nothing is hidden.

The full page

Everything lives in one file. The <canvas> gets an explicit width and height (these are the bitmap's pixel dimensions, not CSS size). We grab it by id and ask for the '2d' context — the object that holds every drawing command.

index.html
<!DOCTYPE html> <html lang='en'> <head> <meta charset='utf-8'> <title>My Canvas</title> <meta name='viewport' content='width=device-width, initial-scale=1'> </head> <body style='margin:0; background:#111;'> <canvas id='game' width='640' height='360'></canvas> <script> const canvas = document.getElementById('game'); const ctx = canvas.getContext('2d'); // the 2D drawing API // Fill the whole canvas with a background color ctx.fillStyle = '#1d2b53'; ctx.fillRect(0, 0, canvas.width, canvas.height); // A red square ctx.fillStyle = '#ff004d'; ctx.fillRect(40, 40, 80, 80); // A green circle ctx.fillStyle = '#00e436'; ctx.beginPath(); ctx.arc(300, 180, 50, 0, Math.PI * 2); ctx.fill(); // Some text ctx.fillStyle = '#fff'; ctx.font = '24px sans-serif'; ctx.fillText('Hello, canvas!', 380, 60); </script> </body> </html>

Coordinate system

The canvas origin (0, 0) is the top-left corner. X grows to the right, Y grows downward. This trips up everyone coming from math class — moving an object "up" means decreasing its Y. fillRect(x, y, w, h) draws from the top-left corner of the rectangle.

CallWhat it draws
ctx.fillStyle = '#rrggbb'Sets the fill color for the next shapes
ctx.fillRect(x,y,w,h)A filled rectangle
ctx.strokeRect(x,y,w,h)An outlined rectangle
ctx.beginPath() / arc / fillA filled circle or curve
ctx.fillText(str,x,y)Text, where y is the baseline
The script goes after the canvas.
The browser parses top to bottom. If your <script> runs before the <canvas> exists, getElementById returns null. Putting the script at the end of <body> (or wrapping it in a load handler) avoids this.
Don't size the canvas with CSS.
Setting width/height in CSS stretches the bitmap and blurs it. Set them as HTML attributes (or via canvas.width = 640 in JS) to control the actual pixel grid. CSS sizing is only for the display box.

How to run it

Save as index.html and double-click. That's it — no server needed for static drawing. Later, when you load images and audio, browsers block file:// access for security, so you'll run a tiny local server (npx serve or VS Code's Live Server). Get in that habit early.

Inspect with DevTools.
Press F12, open the Console. Any typo throws an error there with a line number. If your canvas is blank, the Console almost always tells you why.
🎮 Build This: Static Scene

A single canvas page that paints a small scene.

  1. Create index.html with a 640×360 canvas and a script at the end of the body.
  2. Grab the context with getContext('2d') and fill the background.
  3. Draw at least three shapes: a rectangle, a circle, and a line of text.
  4. Open it in a browser. Then change a color and refresh to confirm your edit loop works.

title: The requestAnimationFrame Loop & Delta Time
html:
Why a loop, not a one-shot script

Last lesson drew once and stopped. A game must repeat forever: read input, update the world, render, repeat — many times per second. That repetition is the game loop, the beating heart of every game ever made.

The browser gives us requestAnimationFrame (rAF) to drive it. You hand rAF a function; the browser calls it right before the next screen repaint — typically 60 times a second, or 144 on a high-refresh display. Crucially, rAF passes your function a timestamp (in milliseconds since page load). You schedule the next frame from inside the callback, creating a self-perpetuating loop.

Delta time: the single most important concept

If you move a box 5 pixels every frame, it travels 300 px/sec on a 60Hz monitor but 720 px/sec on a 144Hz monitor. Your game would literally run faster on better hardware. The fix is delta time (dt): the number of seconds that elapsed since the last frame. You multiply all motion by dt, so speeds are expressed in pixels per second and stay identical on any display.

game.js
const canvas = document.getElementById('game'); const ctx = canvas.getContext('2d'); const box = { x: 50, y: 150, w: 40, h: 40, speed: 200 }; // px/sec let last = 0; function loop(now) { const dt = (now - last) / 1000; // ms -> seconds last = now; update(dt); render(); requestAnimationFrame(loop); // schedule the next frame } function update(dt) { box.x += box.speed * dt; // frame-rate independent! if (box.x > canvas.width) { box.x = canvas.width; box.speed *= -1; } if (box.x < 0) { box.x = 0; box.speed *= -1; } } function render() { ctx.fillStyle = '#1d2b53'; ctx.fillRect(0, 0, canvas.width, canvas.height); // clear ctx.fillStyle = '#ffec27'; ctx.fillRect(box.x, box.y, box.w, box.h); } requestAnimationFrame(loop); // kick it off

The first-frame gotcha

On the very first call, last is 0 but now might be 3000, giving a dt of 3 seconds and a box that teleports. Two common fixes: initialize last on the first frame, or clamp dt to a maximum.

safe-dt.js
let dt = (now - last) / 1000; last = now; if (dt > 0.05) dt = 0.05; // clamp: never step more than 50ms at once

Clamping also protects you when the player switches tabs — the browser pauses rAF, and on return you'd otherwise get a giant dt that flings objects across the screen or through walls.

Clear every frame

The canvas keeps whatever you last drew. If you don't clear it, the box leaves a trail. Either fillRect the whole background (also sets your backdrop) or use ctx.clearRect(0, 0, canvas.width, canvas.height) for transparency.

TermMeaning
nowTimestamp rAF passes in, in ms
dtSeconds since the previous frame
speed × dtDistance moved this frame
FPSFrames per second; 1/dt
Rule: every movement multiplies by dt.
Position, rotation, timers, spawn rates — if it changes over time, multiply by dt. The instant you write x += 5 with no dt, you've coupled your game to one specific refresh rate.
Show your FPS.
While developing, draw Math.round(1/dt) in a corner with fillText. It's the quickest way to spot a performance regression.
🎮 Build This: Bouncing Box

A rectangle that glides across the canvas and bounces off both walls, identical on any monitor.

  1. Set up the rAF loop with dt in seconds, clamped to 0.05.
  2. Give the box x, speed (px/sec), and update x += speed * dt.
  3. Flip speed when it hits the left or right edge.
  4. Clear and redraw each frame. Add an FPS readout in the corner.

title: Drawing Shapes, Images & Sprites
html:
From shapes to pictures

You can build a whole game with just rectangles and circles — Pong needs nothing more. But real games use images: a player drawn from a PNG, backgrounds, particle textures. The canvas draws images with ctx.drawImage, and the catch is timing — an image isn't ready to draw until it has finished loading.

Loading an image correctly

Create an Image object, set its src, and listen for onload. Until that fires, the image has zero width and drawImage silently draws nothing. The safe pattern is to track loaded assets and only start the loop once they're ready.

assets.js
const ball = new Image(); ball.src = 'ball.png'; ball.onload = () => { requestAnimationFrame(loop); // start only after it loads }; ball.onerror = () => console.error('Could not load ball.png'); // later, inside render(): ctx.drawImage(ball, x, y); // natural size ctx.drawImage(ball, x, y, 32, 32); // scaled to 32x32
file:// blocks images.
If you double-click your HTML, the browser may refuse to load local images for security (CORS). Run a local server: npx serve in the project folder, then open the localhost URL it prints. This is the moment a static server becomes mandatory.

The nine-argument drawImage

The most powerful form crops a sub-rectangle out of a source image and draws it scaled. This is the foundation of spritesheets (lesson 6.7): drawImage(img, sx, sy, sw, sh, dx, dy, dw, dh) — source rect first, destination rect second.

Build Pong's visuals

Pong is just three rectangles and one ball, redrawn each frame. We'll lay out the objects now and add motion and input next lesson. Notice everything is data in plain objects — that's all a game state really is.

pong-draw.js
const W = canvas.width, H = canvas.height; const left = { x: 20, y: H/2 - 40, w: 12, h: 80 }; const right = { x: W-32, y: H/2 - 40, w: 12, h: 80 }; const ballPos = { x: W/2, y: H/2, r: 8 }; let scoreL = 0, scoreR = 0; function render() { ctx.fillStyle = '#000'; ctx.fillRect(0, 0, W, H); // dashed center line ctx.fillStyle = '#333'; for (let y = 0; y < H; y += 24) ctx.fillRect(W/2 - 2, y, 4, 12); // paddles + ball, all white ctx.fillStyle = '#fff'; ctx.fillRect(left.x, left.y, left.w, left.h); ctx.fillRect(right.x, right.y, right.w, right.h); ctx.beginPath(); ctx.arc(ballPos.x, ballPos.y, ballPos.r, 0, Math.PI * 2); ctx.fill(); // score ctx.font = '32px monospace'; ctx.textAlign = 'center'; ctx.fillText(scoreL, W/2 - 60, 48); ctx.fillText(scoreR, W/2 + 60, 48); }
PropertyEffect
ctx.textAlign'left', 'center', 'right' relative to x
ctx.textBaseline'top', 'middle', 'alphabetic'
ctx.globalAlpha0–1 transparency for following draws
ctx.save() / ctx.restore()Push/pop the style + transform state
Crisp pixels.
For pixel-art games, scaled images look blurry by default. Set ctx.imageSmoothingEnabled = false once after getting the context to keep hard edges when you scale up sprites.
Draw order is paint order.
The canvas has no z-index. Whatever you draw last sits on top. Render background first, then game objects, then UI/score last so they're never hidden.
🎮 Build This: Pong (visuals)

Render the full Pong board — it won't move yet, but every piece is on screen.

  1. Define left, right paddle objects and a ball object with x, y, r.
  2. In render(), clear to black, draw the dashed center line, paddles, and ball in white.
  3. Draw the two scores centered near the top.
  4. Optionally load a ball.png via onload and swap the circle for drawImage — run it through npx serve.

title: Keyboard, Mouse & Touch Input
html:
Events vs. polling

The browser fires events — keydown, mousemove, touchstart — at unpredictable times. But your game loop runs at a fixed rhythm and wants to ask, every frame, "is the up arrow held right now?" The bridge is simple: event handlers write into a state object; the loop reads that object. This is the input buffer pattern and it's how nearly all games handle input.

Keyboard: track held keys

Don't move the paddle inside the keydown handler — key-repeat is laggy and inconsistent. Instead record which keys are down in a keys map and act on it in update.

keyboard.js
const keys = {}; window.addEventListener('keydown', e => { keys[e.code] = true; }); window.addEventListener('keyup', e => { keys[e.code] = false; }); function update(dt) { const v = left.speed * dt; if (keys['KeyW'] || keys['ArrowUp']) left.y -= v; if (keys['KeyS'] || keys['ArrowDown']) left.y += v; // clamp inside the board left.y = Math.max(0, Math.min(H - left.h, left.y)); }
Use e.code, not e.key.
e.code is the physical key ('KeyW', 'ArrowUp') and is layout-independent. e.key returns 'w' vs 'W' depending on Shift and changes on non-QWERTY layouts. For WASD movement always use e.code.
Stop the page from scrolling.
Arrow keys and Space scroll the page by default, jerking your game around. Call e.preventDefault() in keydown for the keys you use — but only those, so normal browser shortcuts still work.

Mouse: convert to canvas coordinates

Mouse events report coordinates relative to the page, not your canvas. You must subtract the canvas's position with getBoundingClientRect(). If the canvas is also CSS-scaled, multiply by the ratio of bitmap size to displayed size.

mouse.js
function canvasPos(clientX, clientY) { const r = canvas.getBoundingClientRect(); return { x: (clientX - r.left) * (canvas.width / r.width), y: (clientY - r.top) * (canvas.height / r.height) }; } canvas.addEventListener('mousemove', e => { const p = canvasPos(e.clientX, e.clientY); left.y = p.y - left.h / 2; // center paddle on cursor });

Touch: one handler for both paddles

Touch events carry a touches list — potentially several fingers at once. We read each touch's position and move whichever paddle is on that side of the screen. touchmove needs preventDefault() (with {passive:false}) or the page pinch-zooms and scrolls.

touch.js
canvas.addEventListener('touchmove', e => { e.preventDefault(); for (const t of e.touches) { const p = canvasPos(t.clientX, t.clientY); const paddle = p.x < W / 2 ? left : right; paddle.y = Math.max(0, Math.min(H - paddle.h, p.y - paddle.h / 2)); } }, { passive: false });
EventUse for
keydown / keyupHeld movement, action buttons
mousemove / mousedownAiming, dragging, clicks
touchstart / touchmoveMobile dragging and taps
pointermoveUnified mouse+touch (one handler)
Handlers set state, the loop reads it.
Never run game logic directly in an event handler — it desyncs from your frame timing. Record intent ("up is held", "pointer at x,y") and resolve it in update(dt).
🎮 Build This: Playable Pong

Make Pong fully playable on desktop and mobile.

  1. Add the keys map; move the left paddle with W/S and the right with the arrow keys, clamped to the board.
  2. Give the ball a velocity; bounce it off the top/bottom walls and off paddles; score when it passes an edge and reset to center.
  3. Add the canvasPos helper and a touchmove handler so each side's finger drags that paddle.
  4. Call preventDefault() on arrow keys and touchmove. Test on your phone via npx serve and your machine's LAN IP.

title: Vectors & Movement
html:
A position is two numbers that belong together

So far we've tracked x and y as loose variables. As games grow you'll want to add, scale, and measure distances between points constantly. A vector — an {x, y} pair — packages those operations. A position is a vector; a velocity is a vector (direction + speed); the difference between two positions is a vector pointing from one to the other.

You don't need a fancy library. A handful of small functions covers 90% of 2D game math.

vec.js
const Vec = { add: (a, b) => ({ x: a.x + b.x, y: a.y + b.y }), sub: (a, b) => ({ x: a.x - b.x, y: a.y - b.y }), scale: (a, s) => ({ x: a.x * s, y: a.y * s }), len: (a) => Math.hypot(a.x, a.y), norm: (a) => { // unit vector (length 1) const l = Math.hypot(a.x, a.y) || 1; return { x: a.x / l, y: a.y / l }; }, fromAngle: (rad, mag = 1) => // direction -> vector ({ x: Math.cos(rad) * mag, y: Math.sin(rad) * mag }) };

Velocity = direction × speed

To move an object, store a velocity vector measured in pixels per second, then each frame add velocity × dt to position. This is exactly the speed * dt rule from lesson 6.2, now in two dimensions. The key insight: velocity already encodes both how fast and which way, so movement is a single line.

move.js
const ball = { pos: { x: W/2, y: H/2 }, vel: { x: 180, y: 120 }, // px/sec, heading down-right r: 7 }; function update(dt) { ball.pos = Vec.add(ball.pos, Vec.scale(ball.vel, dt)); // bounce off top/bottom by flipping the y component if (ball.pos.y < ball.r) { ball.pos.y = ball.r; ball.vel.y *= -1; } if (ball.pos.y > H - ball.r) { ball.pos.y = H - ball.r; ball.vel.y *= -1; } }

Launching at an angle

To serve the ball in a random upward direction, pick an angle and build the velocity from it. Angles in JS are in radians (2π = full circle), and remember Y points down, so a negative Y component goes up.

serve.js
function serve() { const speed = 260; // angle between -45 and -135 deg (upward fan), in radians const a = (-45 - Math.random() * 90) * Math.PI / 180; ball.pos = { x: W/2, y: H/2 }; ball.vel = Vec.fromAngle(a, speed); }

Keeping a constant speed

When you bounce by flipping a component the speed stays the same. But if you ever add influence (like steering the ball based on where it hits a paddle), the vector can drift faster or slower. Re-normalize and re-scale to lock the magnitude: ball.vel = Vec.scale(Vec.norm(ball.vel), speed).

OperationMeaning
Vec.add(pos, Vec.scale(vel, dt))Step forward one frame
Vec.len(Vec.sub(a, b))Distance between two points
Vec.norm(v)Same direction, length 1
Vec.fromAngle(rad, speed)Velocity from a heading
Normalize diagonals.
If a paddle moves with vel = {x: s, y: s} on diagonals it travels s·√2 ≈ 1.41× faster than along an axis. Normalize the input direction before scaling by speed so diagonal movement isn't faster.
Don't over-engineer.
Plain {x, y} objects and these helpers are enough for everything in this part. Reach for a class or library only when profiling says object churn is a real cost.
🎮 Build This: Breakout (core)

Lay out Breakout and get the ball flying with a velocity vector.

  1. Add the Vec helpers. Give the ball pos and vel; move it with Vec.add(pos, Vec.scale(vel, dt)).
  2. Bounce the ball off the top and side walls by flipping the matching velocity component.
  3. Build a grid of brick objects (rows × columns) with x, y, w, h, and an alive flag; draw them in rows of different colors.
  4. Add a paddle that moves left/right with the keys, clamped to the canvas. Use serve() to launch the ball at a random upward angle.

title: Collision Detection
html:
Two shapes you'll use forever

Nearly every 2D collision in arcade games reduces to two tests: rectangle vs. rectangle (AABB — axis-aligned bounding box) and circle vs. rectangle. They're cheap, exact for these shapes, and easy to reason about. We'll write both and use them to finish Breakout.

Rectangle vs. rectangle (AABB)

Two axis-aligned rectangles overlap only if they overlap on both axes. The test is four comparisons. The mental model: they miss if A is entirely left of B, or entirely right, or above, or below — so they hit when none of those is true.

aabb.js
function aabb(a, b) { return a.x < b.x + b.w && a.x + a.w > b.x && a.y < b.y + b.h && a.y + a.h > b.y; }

Circle vs. rectangle

The trick is to find the point on the rectangle closest to the circle's center by clamping the center into the rectangle's bounds. If that closest point is within one radius of the center, they collide. This is more accurate than treating the ball as a box and matters for corner hits.

circle-rect.js
function circleRect(c, r) { // c={x,y,r} r={x,y,w,h} const cx = Math.max(r.x, Math.min(c.x, r.x + r.w)); const cy = Math.max(r.y, Math.min(c.y, r.y + r.h)); const dx = c.x - cx, dy = c.y - cy; return dx * dx + dy * dy < c.r * c.r; // compare squared distance }
Compare squared distances.
Math.sqrt is relatively slow and unnecessary for a yes/no test. Comparing dx*dx + dy*dy against r*r gives the same answer faster. Reach for the actual distance only when you need the real length.

Responding: which way to bounce?

Detecting a hit is half the job — you must respond believably. For the ball hitting a brick, decide whether it struck a vertical or horizontal side by comparing the overlap on each axis, then flip only that velocity component. A simple, reliable heuristic: compare how far the ball's center is from the brick's center on each axis relative to their combined half-sizes.

breakout-collide.js
function hitBrick(brick) { brick.alive = false; score += 10; // brick center vs ball center to pick bounce axis const bx = brick.x + brick.w / 2; const by = brick.y + brick.h / 2; const dx = (ball.pos.x - bx) / (brick.w / 2); const dy = (ball.pos.y - by) / (brick.h / 2); if (Math.abs(dx) > Math.abs(dy)) ball.vel.x *= -1; // side hit else ball.vel.y *= -1; // top/bottom hit } function update(dt) { ball.pos = Vec.add(ball.pos, Vec.scale(ball.vel, dt)); // walls handled earlier... for (const b of bricks) { if (b.alive && circleRect(ball.pos, b)) { hitBrick(b); break; } } // paddle: steer the ball by where it lands if (circleRect(ball.pos, paddle) && ball.vel.y > 0) { const hit = (ball.pos.x - (paddle.x + paddle.w/2)) / (paddle.w/2); // -1..1 const speed = Vec.len(ball.vel); ball.vel = Vec.scale(Vec.norm({ x: hit, y: -1 }), speed); } }
Push out after bouncing.
If you only flip velocity, a fast ball can stay overlapping the brick for several frames and flip repeatedly, jittering or sticking. After a hit, nudge the ball back outside the brick (set its position to the surface) so it cleanly separates. The break after the first brick hit also prevents clearing two bricks in one frame.
Tunneling: fast objects skip through thin walls.
If vel × dt exceeds an object's thickness, the ball can teleport past it without ever overlapping. Cap ball speed, keep bricks/walls thick enough, or sub-step the movement (move in several small increments per frame) for very fast objects.
🎮 Build This: Breakout (finished)

Make the ball clear bricks and bounce off the paddle.

  1. Add circleRect. Each frame, test the ball against every alive brick; on a hit, kill the brick, add score, and bounce.
  2. Decide the bounce axis from the ball-vs-brick center comparison; nudge the ball outside the brick.
  3. Test the ball against the paddle and steer it left/right based on where it lands, keeping speed constant.
  4. Lose a life when the ball falls below the paddle; show "You win" when all bricks are gone.

title: Spritesheet Animation & Web Audio
html:
Animation is just cropping over time

A character's walk cycle is a strip of frames packed into one image — a spritesheet. To animate, you draw a different sub-rectangle each tick using the nine-argument drawImage from lesson 6.3. An animation timer advances the frame index at a fixed rate (independent of your render FPS, thanks to dt).

anim.js
const sheet = new Image(); sheet.src = 'bird.png'; // 3 frames, each 34x24, in a horizontal strip const FRAME_W = 34, FRAME_H = 24, FRAMES = 3; let frame = 0, frameTimer = 0; const FRAME_TIME = 0.08; // seconds per frame function updateAnim(dt) { frameTimer += dt; if (frameTimer >= FRAME_TIME) { frameTimer -= FRAME_TIME; frame = (frame + 1) % FRAMES; // loop 0,1,2,0,1,2... } } function drawBird(x, y) { ctx.drawImage( sheet, frame * FRAME_W, 0, FRAME_W, FRAME_H, // source crop x, y, FRAME_W, FRAME_H // destination ); }
Accumulate, don't reset.
Write frameTimer -= FRAME_TIME (not = 0). Subtracting keeps any leftover time, so on slow frames the animation can advance multiple frames and stays in sync with wall-clock time.

Rotating a sprite

To tilt the bird based on its velocity, wrap the draw in save/translate/rotate/restore. translate moves the origin to the bird's center, rotate spins around it, and you draw offset by half its size so it rotates about its middle.

rotate.js
ctx.save(); ctx.translate(bird.x, bird.y); ctx.rotate(Math.max(-0.5, Math.min(1, bird.vy / 600))); // tilt with fall speed ctx.drawImage(sheet, frame*FRAME_W, 0, FRAME_W, FRAME_H, -FRAME_W/2, -FRAME_H/2, FRAME_W, FRAME_H); ctx.restore();

Sound with the Web Audio API

The simplest sound is an <audio> element, but it can't overlap with itself and has latency. The Web Audio API is the real tool: decode a sound once into an AudioBuffer, then fire a fresh, cheap BufferSource every time you want to play it — overlapping freely with near-zero lag.

audio.js
const audioCtx = new (window.AudioContext || window.webkitAudioContext)(); const buffers = {}; async function loadSound(name, url) { const res = await fetch(url); const data = await res.arrayBuffer(); buffers[name] = await audioCtx.decodeAudioData(data); } function play(name, volume = 1) { const buf = buffers[name]; if (!buf) return; const src = audioCtx.createBufferSource(); const gain = audioCtx.createGain(); gain.gain.value = volume; src.buffer = buf; src.connect(gain).connect(audioCtx.destination); src.start(0); // one-shot; can overlap with itself } Promise.all([ loadSound('flap','flap.wav'), loadSound('score','score.wav') ]);
Autoplay is blocked until a gesture.
Browsers create the AudioContext in a suspended state and won't make sound until the user interacts. On your first keydown/click/touchstart, call audioCtx.resume(). Without this your game is silent and you'll waste an hour wondering why.
ConceptRole
AudioContextThe audio engine; create one and reuse it
decodeAudioDataTurns a file into a reusable buffer
BufferSourceA one-shot player; make a new one each play
GainNodeVolume control between source and output
Keep effects short and decode once.
Decode every sound at load time, never per play. Use small WAV/OGG files for SFX. For background music, looping an <audio> element is fine and lighter on memory.
🎮 Build This: Flappy-style Game

An animated bird that flaps through scrolling pipes, with sound.

  1. Apply gravity to the bird's vy each frame; on flap (space/tap) set vy to a negative jump value and play('flap').
  2. Animate the wings from a 3-frame spritesheet and tilt the sprite by its fall speed.
  3. Spawn pipe pairs at the right edge with a random gap, scroll them left with dt, and recycle them off-screen.
  4. Score and play('score') when a pipe passes; collide with pipes/ground using AABB. Call audioCtx.resume() on the first input.

title: Capstone: A State Machine & Deploying to the Web
html:
Why a state machine

A real game isn't always "playing." It shows a menu, plays, pauses, and ends. Cramming all that into one update with a forest of if flags becomes unmaintainable fast. A finite state machine (FSM) keeps exactly one state active at a time, each with its own update and render, and explicit transitions between them. Your loop simply delegates to the current state.

states.js
let current; function setState(s) { current = s; if (current.enter) current.enter(); // reset on entry } const Menu = { enter() {}, update(dt) { if (keys['Space']) setState(Play); }, render() { drawCenteredText('ARCADE', 64, H/2 - 30); drawCenteredText('Press Space to start', 20, H/2 + 30); } }; const Play = { enter() { resetGame(); }, update(dt) { movePlayer(dt); moveEnemies(dt); checkCollisions(); if (lives <= 0) setState(GameOver); }, render() { drawWorld(); drawHud(); } }; const GameOver = { enter() { saveHighScore(score); }, update(dt) { if (keys['Space']) setState(Menu); }, render() { drawWorld(); drawCenteredText('GAME OVER', 56, H/2 - 20); drawCenteredText('Score: ' + score, 24, H/2 + 30); } }; function loop(now) { const dt = Math.min((now - last) / 1000, 0.05); last = now; current.update(dt); ctx.clearRect(0, 0, W, H); current.render(); requestAnimationFrame(loop); } setState(Menu); last = performance.now(); requestAnimationFrame(loop);
enter() is where you reset.
Put initialization in each state's enter() — reset score and spawn enemies in Play.enter(), save the high score in GameOver.enter(). Transitions stay clean because each state owns its own setup.

Persisting a high score

localStorage survives page reloads — perfect for a high score. It only stores strings, so parse on read.

highscore.js
function getHigh() { return Number(localStorage.getItem('hi') || 0); } function saveHighScore(s) { if (s > getHigh()) localStorage.setItem('hi', s); }

Preparing to ship

Your game is already a static site — HTML, JS, images, audio. No backend, no build step required. Before deploying, tidy it up.

  • Put everything in one folder with index.html at its root.
  • Use relative paths ('bird.png', not '/bird.png') so it works in a subfolder.
  • Make the canvas scale responsively with CSS (max-width:100%) while keeping its bitmap size fixed.
  • Test the whole thing through npx serve one final time, including a hard refresh to catch missing assets.

Deploying to itch.io

itch.io hosts HTML5 games for free and is the standard for indie/jam games.

  1. Zip your project folder so index.html is at the top level of the zip (not inside a nested folder).
  2. On itch.io: Upload new project → set Kind of project to HTML.
  3. Upload the zip and tick This file will be played in the browser.
  4. Set the viewport dimensions to your canvas size (e.g. 640×360), enable fullscreen, and save.
  5. Set visibility to Public and play it from the page.
Any static host works.
The same folder deploys to GitHub Pages (push to a repo, enable Pages), Netlify or Vercel (drag-and-drop the folder), or Cloudflare Pages — all free. itch.io just adds a games audience and a built-in browser player.
The zip-root mistake.
If itch.io shows a blank frame, you almost certainly zipped a folder containing index.html instead of zipping its contents. itch serves the zip's root — index.html must be right there. Re-zip from inside the folder.
Mute by default, license your assets.
Many players browse with sound off-screen; start muted or require a click to enable audio. And only ship art/audio you made or that's licensed for use — credit sources in your itch description.
🎮 Build This: Arcade Capstone + Deploy

Ship a small arcade game with proper states, live on the web.

  1. Pick a simple arcade loop (dodge falling blocks, shoot invaders, collect coins under a timer).
  2. Implement Menu, Play, and GameOver states with the FSM above; delegate update/render to the current state.
  3. Track score and persist a high score in localStorage; reuse delta time, vectors, collision, animation, and audio from this part.
  4. Test through a local server with relative paths, then zip with index.html at the root and upload to itch.io as an HTML project. Share the link.
